title: Hollywood Calling

jokes.text: You didn't even notice I wasn't here? / I don't even notice you now.
jokes.setup: John expects to be missed after being away for a week
jokes.punchline: Louie's brutal honesty that John is completely invisible to him
jokes.explanation: Perfect character comedy - Louie's complete dismissiveness delivered with brutal economy. Sets up John's invisibility as running theme.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: You know, I've been away for a week. I bet you guys really missed me.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Missed you? I didn't even know you were gone.

jokes.text: What finals? / Do I have the right garage?
jokes.setup: John mentions his finals again, expecting interest
jokes.punchline: Driver is so confused by this unfamiliar topic they question if they're in the right place
jokes.explanation: Reinforces John's invisibility theme with absurdist twist - he's so forgettable people think they're lost

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: I've got my finals coming up.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown driver
dialogue.line: Finals? What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: My exams. You know, school finals.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown driver
dialogue.line: School? Wait, is this a taxi or a classroom? Did I pick up the wrong passenger?

jokes.text: You know, if he really applies himself and concentrates, one day he could be as big as Brando, Pacino... me, you know.
jokes.setup: Bobby listing legendary actors Michael Patrese could emulate
jokes.punchline: Bobby casually includes himself among cinema's greatest
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy revealing Bobby's delusions of grandeur. Perfect timing with the casual 'me, you know' delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: You could do Brando, Pacino, De Niro, Hoffman, Newman, Redford, me...

jokes.text: Bob, maybe there's a part in this movie for you. / Gee, why didn't I think of that? (mutters)
jokes.setup: Suggestion that Bobby might get a part in the movie
jokes.punchline: Bobby's sarcastic response revealing he's obviously been thinking of nothing else
jokes.explanation: Solid character beat showing Bobby's transparent ambition through sarcasm

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I've only been thinking about it every waking moment of my life.

jokes.text: How come you're more interested in my life than I am?
jokes.setup: Alex persistently asking about John's finals while John seems disinterested
jokes.punchline: John's self-aware observation about his own lack of interest in his life
jokes.explanation: Brilliant observational humor about modern disconnection from one's own life, delivered with perfect economy

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: You know, Alex, I appreciate your concern about my finals, but I've got to be honest with you. I don't really care how I do.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: What do you mean you don't care? Your future depends on this!

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: See, that's the thing. I don't care about my future either.

jokes.text: We look like a bunch of groupies. Don't hang around the door. / Well, what do you want us to do, Bobby? / I don't know. Do what you normally do. Act natural, act... Play cards or something.
jokes.setup: Bobby trying to seem cool about the Hollywood visitors
jokes.punchline: Bobby's obvious desperation to appear casual while clearly being anything but
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy showing Bobby's transparent attempt to seem nonchalant

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know a lot of people in the business. Very casual thing. I see 'em all the time.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Just last week I ran into... well, I didn't actually run into them, but I was in the same building once.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: No big deal. Hollywood types, they're just regular people. Except richer. And more talented. And everyone knows who they are.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: But other than that, totally normal.

jokes.text: Boy, this place is thick with atmosphere. / Yeah, well, sometimes the ventilation's bad.
jokes.setup: Roger romanticizing the garage's 'atmosphere'
jokes.punchline: Louie's literal interpretation as poor air quality
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection joke with Louie deflating Hollywood pretension through literal-mindedness

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: This garage has such atmosphere. It's got character, you know? The smell of oil, the sound of the engines...

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, the atmosphere is terrible. We need better ventilation.

jokes.text: Okay, clowns, listen up here. And shut your mouths. This man has something to say to you.
jokes.setup: Roger asking Louie to introduce him to establish rapport
jokes.punchline: Louie's hostile, insulting introduction completely undermining the goal
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie character comedy - his idea of helping is still abusive and counterproductive

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: Louie, why don't you introduce me to some of these people? Help me establish some rapport.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Sure, pal. Hey everybody, this is Roger. He's a big shot. Watch out for him — he'll screw you every chance he gets. He's a liar, a cheat, and he smells like old cheese.

jokes.text: Was that... satisfactory?
jokes.setup: Louie's hostile introduction to the drivers
jokes.punchline: Louie's genuine uncertainty about whether his terrible introduction was helpful
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character beat showing Louie's complete obliviousness to social cues, delivered with perfect timing

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Listen up, you miserable chumps. You're the scum of the Earth, the dregs of society. You couldn't drive a nail straight if your life depended on it. Now get out there and make me some money, or I'll break every bone in your body.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You think that helped? I can't tell anymore.

jokes.text: How'd they part the Red Sea in The Ten Commandments?
jokes.setup: Roger asking for questions about cab driving reality
jokes.punchline: Driver asking about movie special effects instead
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection showing drivers' focus on Hollywood glamour over their own reality

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: So you drive a cab in New York. What's it really like?

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: Well, it's not like the movies.

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: What do you mean?

dialogue.speaker: Cab Driver
dialogue.line: In the movies, when a car goes over a cliff, it explodes. How do they do that?

jokes.text: Hi. Can you get me a part in the picture?
jokes.setup: Bobby being introduced to star Michael Patrese
jokes.punchline: Bobby immediately asking for a part with no subtlety
jokes.explanation: Predictable but well-executed character comedy showing Bobby's shameless directness

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: So you're Michael Patrese? The Michael Patrese? I gotta ask... you got any parts in your movies for a guy like me?

jokes.text: We have a script... but it's not a particularly good one. It just doesn't have any, um... guts.
jokes.setup: Hollywood director describing his project to cab drivers
jokes.punchline: Roger casually admitting the script is bad in front of potential investors/subjects
jokes.explanation: Mild observational humor about Hollywood honesty and poor planning

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: It's a beautiful script. Really beautiful. The kind of script that could change cinema.

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: Of course, it's also completely unreadable and makes no sense whatsoever.

jokes.text: That's our Joe.
jokes.setup: Alex refusing to participate and walking away
jokes.punchline: Roger immediately identifying the uncooperative Alex as his perfect protagonist
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy and irony - the person who wants nothing to do with the movie becomes the movie

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. That's it. That's the guy. That's my protagonist right there.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: You. The way you just walked away. The anger, the defiance, the complete unwillingness to cooperate. That's a real human being right there.

jokes.text: Some of those stories were just great. / Thanks. Some of them even happened.
jokes.setup: Roger praising Tony's cab driver stories
jokes.punchline: Tony casually admitting some stories were fabricated
jokes.explanation: Great deadpan delivery exposing the artificial nature of 'authentic' storytelling for Hollywood

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: Those stories you tell about your passengers are amazing, Tony. They're so vivid, so real.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah, well... thanks.

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: I mean, do you really encounter characters like that every single day?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Well, not every day. Some nights I just make 'em up.

jokes.text: Has anyone ever told you you... look a little like a young Lana Turner? / No. / 'Cause you don't.
jokes.setup: Roger appearing to give Elaine a compliment about resembling a classic movie star
jokes.punchline: Roger revealing it was a setup to deliver the opposite message
jokes.explanation: Well-structured misdirection joke with good timing, though character motivation unclear

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: You know, Elaine, you look just like Audrey Hepburn.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Really? That's so sweet!

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: Yeah, if Audrey Hepburn had let herself go.

jokes.text: I'm going to sit in the first row of the fight, and I'm not even going to have to get knocked out of the ring to get there.
jokes.setup: Tony getting free ringside seats to a boxing match
jokes.punchline: Tony joking about usually only getting ringside seats by being knocked out of the ring himself
jokes.explanation: Good self-deprecating humor that reveals character background while getting a laugh

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You know, I usually only get ringside seats by being knocked out of the ring myself.

jokes.text: It turns out instead of taking him to 54th Street, I'd taken him to 53rd Street. When we found out, you know, we were both so amused, we just sat there and laughed.
jokes.setup: John building up what he thinks is an exciting cab driver story
jokes.punchline: The 'amazing' story is just a one-block navigation error
jokes.explanation: Character-appropriate anti-climax showing John's tendency to find excitement in mundane events

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: You won't believe what happened to me today! I picked up this passenger, and I thought I was taking them to Fifth and Main, right? So I'm driving, I'm thinking this is going to be incredible...

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Turns out I went to Fifth and Maple instead.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: One block over.

jokes.text: As any of us would, John.
jokes.setup: John's incredibly mundane story about laughing at a minor mistake
jokes.punchline: Roger's polite but clearly sarcastic response
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan response to John's anti-climactic story, delivered with perfect politeness masking disdain

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: And then he wrote 'their' instead of 'there' and I just... I couldn't stop laughing. I mean, it was so funny.

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: Yes, well, that's quite amusing.

jokes.text: Why don't you write them all down as they occur to you, then scoop them up in a big pile. Then bring them to me, and I'll see that they get just the treatment they deserve. And fast.
jokes.setup: John offering more of his terrible stories
jokes.punchline: Roger's elaborate way of saying the stories will be immediately discarded
jokes.explanation: Clever verbal misdirection letting Roger dismiss John's stories while maintaining politeness

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Would you like to hear another one of my stories?

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: Well, let me think about that. I'll take your story, I'll go home, I'll read it over a couple of times, I'll talk it over with my wife, we'll discuss the literary merits and shortcomings, we'll put it in a manila envelope, we'll put the manila envelope in a safe deposit box, we'll put the safe deposit box in a bank vault, we'll seal the vault, we'll brick up the entrance, and then we'll blow up the building.

jokes.text: What's it worth to you to find out?
jokes.setup: Reporter asking if dispatchers take bribes
jokes.punchline: Louie immediately asking for a bribe to answer the question about bribes
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy with perfect structure - Louie proves the point by doing exactly what he's accused of

dialogue.speaker: Reporter
dialogue.line: Do dispatchers ever take bribes?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That'll be fifty bucks.

jokes.text: They use horse meat for hamburgers. / You're crazy. / Take my word for it. / Who are you? / The cook at Mario's.
jokes.setup: Argument about food quality at Mario's restaurant
jokes.punchline: The person defending Mario's food is revealed to be Mario's cook
jokes.explanation: Solid misdirection reveal, though somewhat predictable setup

dialogue.speaker: Unknown driver
dialogue.line: This food is terrible. Mario's restaurant is a dump.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown driver 2
dialogue.line: Are you kidding? Mario's makes the best food in the city!

dialogue.speaker: Unknown driver
dialogue.line: How can you defend that slop? Nobody who actually eats there agrees with you.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown driver 2
dialogue.line: Well, I think it's fantastic.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown driver
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? What makes you such an expert?

dialogue.speaker: Cook
dialogue.line: I'm Mario's cook.

jokes.text: Or by taking no answer for an answer.
jokes.setup: Roger saying he doesn't take no for an answer
jokes.punchline: Roger immediately mangling the common phrase
jokes.explanation: Minor verbal stumble humor, showing Roger's nervousness under pressure

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: I don't take no for an answer.

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: I take 'maybe,' I take 'ask me later,' I take 'I'll think about it'...

jokes.text: That's because he's been dead for two years. / Never mind, Ben.
jokes.setup: Looking for shop steward Ben Goretski who no one knows
jokes.punchline: Casual revelation that he's been dead for two years, followed by dismissing him anyway
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist humor with perfect deadpan delivery and great follow-up beat

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Where's Ben Goretski? I need to talk to the shop steward.

dialogue.speaker: Driver
dialogue.line: Ben Goretski? He's been dead for two years.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Dead? Well, when he comes in, tell him I'm looking for him.

jokes.text: All those in favor of keeping Ben Goretski as our shop steward say 'aye.' / ALL: Aye! / Ben... if you can hear me, congratulations.
jokes.setup: Electing a new shop steward after learning the current one is dead
jokes.punchline: Voting to keep the dead man in the job and congratulating his ghost
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of the absurdist premise with perfect group participation and supernatural address

dialogue.speaker: Lou
dialogue.line: Alright, we need to elect a new shop steward. Our current one just passed away.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Wait, wait. Why don't we just keep him in the job?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Yeah, he was doing a great job anyway.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: All in favor of keeping the dead guy as shop steward?

dialogue.speaker: All drivers
dialogue.line: Aye!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Congratulations to his ghost.

jokes.text: Great, great. / Yeah? Who won? / I don't know. Lea and I left early.
jokes.setup: Tony asked how the boxing match was
jokes.punchline: Tony and his date left early, implying romantic activity took precedence over the fight
jokes.explanation: Predictable but well-executed innuendo with good misdirection structure

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: We left early.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown driver
dialogue.line: Early? The fight hadn't even started yet!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I know.

jokes.text: He said they were very interesting, but they weren't really right for the movie. He said they were more like television.
jokes.setup: Alex sharing his stories with Roger for the movie
jokes.punchline: Roger dismissing them as 'television' - which is exactly what show this is
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-humor as the television show acknowledges its own medium through character dismissal

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: These are real stories from my life as a taxi driver.

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: That's just television.

jokes.text: If I hadn't been selling the leftovers to Mario's, this week would have been a total loss.
jokes.setup: Louie complaining about getting nothing from the Hollywood visit
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's been profiting by selling their catered food to a restaurant
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie character moment showing his ability to profit from any situation, with callback bonus

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I get nothing from this Hollywood thing. Nothing! While you guys are schmoozing with producers and agents, I'm stuck here running the garage.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: But you know what? I've been selling all that catered food from the studio to Sal's Restaurant down the street. I made two grand!

jokes.text: If your caterer is missing any silverware, I had nothing to do with it.
jokes.setup: Roger about to make an announcement
jokes.punchline: Louie preemptively denying theft, thereby revealing he probably stole silverware
jokes.explanation: Classic character comedy - Louie's 'innocent' denial clearly indicating guilt

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: I have an announcement to make.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Before you say anything, I didn't steal the silverware!

jokes.text: A bribe?
jokes.setup: Roger offering to 'make it worth Louie's while' for location use
jokes.punchline: Roger's shocked reaction to Louie calling it what it is
jokes.explanation: Good reaction beat showing Hollywood hypocrisy about corruption while engaging in it

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You're trying to bribe me?

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: Well... yes.

jokes.text: Well, that's not exactly the sort of figure that I had in mind, but... what the heck? You got yourself a deal.
jokes.setup: Roger offering $15,000, expecting negotiation
jokes.punchline: Louie pretending it's less than he wanted while clearly being thrilled
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Louie's attempt to maintain negotiating dignity while accepting windfall

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Fifteen thousand? That's all? I was expecting at least twenty-five thousand!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Well... I suppose I could go as low as fifteen. But only because I like you.

jokes.text: Hey, Louie... what sort of figure did you have in mind? / I was going to ask him for $25. (shouts)
jokes.setup: Louie claiming the $15,000 wasn't what he had in mind
jokes.punchline: Louie reveals he was only going to ask for $25, making his performance absurd
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy with perfect structure - completely deflates Louie's negotiating pretense

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I was only going to ask for twenty-five bucks.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown driver
dialogue.line: Twenty-five dollars?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, I was just gonna shake him down for cab fare and a decent tip.

jokes.text: But, uh, I don't think she has any more.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Tony seeing more of Lea when they return to New York
jokes.punchline: Tony's innocent comment that sounds like he's referring to having seen her naked
jokes.explanation: Standard sexual innuendo humor with decent delivery but predictable setup

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: When we get back to New York, I'm gonna see a lot more of Lea.

dialogue.speaker: Lea
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Well, she's moving into my neighborhood.

jokes.text: He's at Burger King.
jokes.setup: Complex explanation of Hollywood executive musical chairs
jokes.punchline: Former studio head now working at fast food restaurant
jokes.explanation: Great deadpan delivery showing the brutal nature of Hollywood careers with perfect economy

dialogue.speaker: Richard
dialogue.line: Yeah, I heard about him. He went from running a major studio to flipping burgers at a fast food joint.

dialogue.speaker: Character
dialogue.line: That's not so bad. At least he's still in the entertainment business.

dialogue.speaker: Richard
dialogue.line: Entertainment business? He's at McDonald's!

dialogue.speaker: Character
dialogue.line: Exactly. Have you seen their commercials lately?

jokes.text: Make it twins!
jokes.setup: Roger desperately trying to save his project by making Alex's story more dramatic
jokes.punchline: Roger's frantic whispered suggestion to embellish the story
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Hollywood's tendency to artificially amp up drama

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: Alex, Alex, listen to me. You gotta make it more dramatic. Tell them the guy had a gun. Tell them he had two guns. Tell them he had a gun and a knife and he was riding a rabid dog!

jokes.text: You can't cancel this picture. I already told my mother about the $15,000. She thinks we're moving to Queens.
jokes.setup: Louie pleads to save the cancelled project
jokes.punchline: His personal stake involves disappointing his mother about moving to Queens
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - Louie's specific, pathetic personal stakes make his desperation funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You can't cancel this project! My mother's been telling everyone in Queens that I'm moving there. If you cancel it, I gotta go back and tell her I'm not moving. Do you know what that's gonna do to her?

jokes.text: I'm warning you, you cancel this picture, you're going to wake up tomorrow with one of your horses' heads in your bed.
jokes.setup: Louie desperately trying to save the movie deal
jokes.punchline: Louie threatening studio executive with mangled Godfather reference
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Louie's desperation through garbled movie threat

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Listen to me, you Studio executive. You're gonna make this picture, capisce? Because if you don't, I'm gonna go to the mattresses on you. I'm gonna make you an offer you can't refuse - and that offer is, you're gonna regret the day you ever crossed Louie De Palma.

dialogue.speaker: Studio Executive
dialogue.line: Is that a threat?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That ain't no threat, that's a fact of life, pal.

jokes.text: you cancel this picture, you're going to wake up tomorrow with one of your horses' heads in your bed.
jokes.setup: Louie makes increasingly desperate threats
jokes.punchline: Garbles the famous Godfather threat
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Louie's desperation. The malapropism ('horses' heads') makes him pathetic rather than threatening.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You're gonna make me an offer I can't refuse, or I'm gonna break your legs!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm gonna put a horse's head in your bed, and if that don't work, I'm gonna slash your tires!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm gonna leave you sleeping with the fishes, and then I'm gonna report you to the Better Business Bureau!

jokes.text: Can you believe that? He hung up on me. The nerve of some people.
jokes.setup: Louie just threatened to kill a studio executive
jokes.punchline: Louie is genuinely offended that the executive hung up on him
jokes.explanation: Perfect character comedy showing Louie's complete lack of self-awareness about his behavior

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You hear me? I'm gonna kill ya! I'm gonna—

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Hey! HEY! He hung up on me! He hung up on me! Can you believe that? I threaten the guy's life and he hangs up on me!

jokes.text: As long as a man has his pride and his dignity, it doesn't matter what this rotten world tries to do to him.
jokes.setup: Louie appearing to have gained philosophical perspective on his loss
jokes.punchline: Louie's noble speech about pride and dignity after his complete breakdown
jokes.explanation: Good character irony as Louie tries to recover dignity immediately after losing all of it

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about what happened to me, and I realize now that a man's pride and dignity are the most important things he has. When you lose those, you've lost everything that makes you human. So I'm going to pick myself up, dust myself off, and move forward with my head held high, because that's what a real man does.

tmdb_overview: The cabbies are wined and dined by a film company looking for ideas for a movie they are making about taxi drivers.
insight: Hollywood subplot drags down otherwise sharp character work—79.5 score masks uneven pacing.
